lesson.introduction: Class 9 mathematics introduces you to probability, statistics, and linear equations — powerful tools that professional analysts, insurers, and economists use daily. Let's see how your classroom math connects to real financial decision-making.

sections.title: Statistics → Reading Financial Reports
sections.content: The mean, median, and mode you calculate for a grouped data table are the same measures financial analysts use when studying stock prices, employee salaries, and economic surveys. The median household income in India (₹1.2 lakh/year) is exactly a median calculation from survey data.
sections.realWorldConnection: RBI (Reserve Bank of India) publishes Consumer Price Index using statistical means and medians. Your statistics chapter IS economic literacy.
sections.keyInsight: Mean, Median, Mode — these three numbers tell the story of any dataset, from exam scores to stock markets.

sections.title: Probability → Insurance and Risk
sections.content: If a bag has 10 balls (6 red, 4 blue), P(red) = 6/10 = 0.6. Insurance companies use EXACTLY this logic: if 6 out of 10 cars get minor accidents per year, and repairs cost ₹20,000 each, the expected cost is 0.6 × 20,000 = ₹12,000/year — so premiums are set above this.
sections.realWorldConnection: IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory Authority of India) mandates that insurance pricing be based on actuarial probability calculations — using your Class 9 probability formula.
sections.keyInsight: Every insurance premium you or your family pays is a probability calculation.

sections.title: Linear Equations → Break-Even Analysis
sections.content: A canteen has fixed costs of ₹5,000/month and earns ₹30 per meal. When does it break even? 30x = 5000 → x = 167 meals. This is a linear equation in one variable — the 'break-even point' every business calculates before launching.
sections.realWorldConnection: Shark Tank India's entrepreneurs calculate break-even in every pitch. Your linear equation is their business model.
sections.keyInsight: Break-even = Total Fixed Costs ÷ Profit per Unit. Linear equations drive business decisions.

sections.title: Compound Interest Deep Dive
sections.content: ₹10,000 invested at 12% compound interest (typical equity mutual fund average) doubles in 6 years (Rule of 72: 72/12=6). After 30 years, ₹10,000 becomes ₹10,000 × (1.12)^30 ≈ ₹3,00,000. This is the magic of compounding — why Warren Buffett says 'start early'.
sections.realWorldConnection: SIP in a Nifty Index Fund has delivered ~12-13% CAGR over 20 years. ₹5,000/month for 20 years ≈ ₹50 lakh at 12% CAGR.
sections.keyInsight: Compound Interest is 'the eighth wonder of the world' — Albert Einstein. It also appears in your Class 9 math book.

practicalAddOn.title: Build a Simple Budget Tracker Spreadsheet
practicalAddOn.description: Create a monthly budget tracker using Google Sheets or Excel that uses mean, median, and percentage calculations to analyse your (or a hypothetical family's) expenses.
steps: List 10 expense categories (food, transport, school, etc.)
Calculate mean monthly expense per category using AVERAGE formula
Calculate what % each category is of total income
Identify top 3 categories — could any be reduced?
mathFormulas: Probability P(A) = favourable outcomes / total outcomes
Break-even: Fixed Cost = (Revenue per unit) × x

agenticChallenge.title: Mutual Fund Simulator
agenticChallenge.description: Pick any 2 real mutual funds from AMFI (amfiindia.com) or Zerodha Coin. Using historical NAV data, calculate the 1-year, 3-year, and 5-year CAGR using the compound interest formula. Compare the two funds. Which would you choose and why?
agenticChallenge.hint: CAGR = [(Ending Value / Beginning Value)^(1/years)] - 1. This is the compound interest formula rearranged for the rate.
agenticChallenge.realWorldConnection: This is literally what a SEBI-registered investment advisor does when recommending mutual funds.
